enumeration.description: Sequential IDs (1, 2, 3...) allow attackers to easily enumerate resources. UUIDs eliminate this vulnerability by using non-predictable identifiers.

gdpr.description: UUIDs support data anonymization requirements by providing identifiers that cannot be easily correlated across systems.

information_leakage.description: Sequential IDs can reveal business metrics (number of users, hosts, etc.). UUIDs prevent this information disclosure.

privacy.description: UUIDs provide non-correlatable identifiers that enhance user privacy and prevent cross-system tracking.
privacy.title: 🕵️ Enhanced Privacy Protection

replay.description: Non-sequential identifiers prevent attackers from predicting valid resource IDs for replay attacks or automated scanning.
replay.title: 🔄 Replay Attack Protection

uuid_architecture.description: SysManage implements UUID-based primary keys across all database tables instead of traditional sequential integer IDs. This architectural decision provides significant security advantages and prevents common attack vectors.

deployment.overview_description: Antivirus deployment in SysManage is a fully automated process that installs and configures the antivirus software appropriate for the target host's operating system.

process.description: The deployment process varies slightly by platform but generally follows these steps:

linux_bsd.step2: ClamAV and related packages are downloaded and installed
linux_bsd.step3: freshclam service is enabled and started (updates virus definitions)
linux_bsd.step4: clamd service is enabled and started (real-time scanning daemon)
linux_bsd.step5: Initial virus definition update is performed
linux_bsd.step6: Agent reports deployment status back to server

macos.step2: freshclam is configured and virus definitions are updated
macos.step3: ClamAV service is started via brew services

process.title: What Happens During Deployment

windows.step1: ClamAV for Windows installer is downloaded
windows.step3: ClamAV Windows service is configured and started

steps.step6: Confirm the deployment in the dialog that appears
steps.step7: Monitor the deployment progress - the antivirus card will update when deployment completes

kvm.creation_desc: When creating a KVM virtual machine through SysManage:
kvm.creation_step1: SysManage downloads the cloud image from the distribution's official source
kvm.creation_step2: A new disk image is created and the cloud image is applied as a backing store
kvm.creation_step3: Cloud-init configuration is generated with hostname, user account, and agent settings
kvm.creation_step4: The VM is defined in libvirt with the specified resources (CPU, memory, disk)
kvm.creation_step5: VM boots and cloud-init configures the system, creates the user, and installs the agent
kvm.creation_step6: The sysmanage-agent service starts and connects to your SysManage server
kvm.creation_step7: The VM appears in the Hosts list pending approval (or auto-approved if selected)
kvm.detect_1: KVM kernel modules are loaded (/dev/kvm exists)
kvm.detect_2: libvirt daemon is running and accessible
kvm.detect_3: The virsh command is available and functional
kvm.detect_4: At least one libvirt network is configured
kvm.detection_desc: SysManage automatically detects KVM capability by checking:
kvm.detection_role: When detected, the host is assigned the KVM Host role and KVM appears as an available virtualization type.
kvm.distributions_desc: KVM VMs can be created using cloud images from distributions where the sysmanage-agent is available:
kvm.field_cpus: vCPUs: Number of virtual CPUs - default is 2
kvm.field_disk: Disk Size: Virtual disk size (e.g., "20G", "50G") - default is 20GB
kvm.field_vm_name: VM Name: The libvirt domain name (must be unique on the host)
kvm.form_desc: When creating KVM VMs, additional configuration options are available:
kvm.lifecycle_delete: Delete: Remove the VM and its storage using virsh undefine --remove-all-storage
kvm.lifecycle_desc: Once created, KVM VMs support the full range of lifecycle operations:
kvm.lifecycle_stop: Stop: Gracefully shutdown the VM using virsh shutdown
kvm.net_dhcp: DHCP: VMs receive IP addresses automatically from the virbr0 bridge (typically 192.168.122.x)
kvm.net_dns: DNS: VMs use the host's DNS servers for name resolution
kvm.net_nat: NAT: Outbound connectivity is provided via NAT through the host
kvm.networking_bridged: For advanced networking, SysManage also supports bridged mode where VMs get IP addresses directly from your network's DHCP server.
kvm.networking_desc: KVM VMs connect to the libvirt default network which provides:
kvm.overview_desc: KVM (Kernel-based Virtual Machine) is a virtualization technology built into the Linux kernel. Combined with QEMU for hardware emulation and libvirt for management, it provides enterprise-grade virtual machine capabilities. SysManage integrates with KVM/libvirt to enable seamless VM creation and lifecycle management from the web interface.
kvm.req_cpu: CPU with hardware virtualization support (Intel VT-x or AMD-V)
kvm.req_kernel: Linux kernel with KVM modules (kvm, kvm_intel or kvm_amd)
kvm.req_network: libvirt default network or custom network bridge configured
kvm.req_user: The sysmanage-agent user must be in the libvirt group
kvm.setup_auto: Alternatively, click "Enable KVM" in the SysManage web interface to automate these setup steps.
kvm.setup_desc: To prepare a host for KVM virtual machine management:
kvm.setup_step2: Install KVM packages: sudo apt install qemu-kvm libvirt-daemon-system (Debian/Ubuntu) or sudo dnf install qemu-kvm libvirt (Fedora/RHEL)
kvm.setup_step3: Enable and start libvirtd: sudo systemctl enable --now libvirtd
kvm.setup_step4: Add agent user to libvirt group: sudo usermod -aG libvirt sysmanage-agent
kvm.setup_step5: Configure the default network: sudo virsh net-autostart default && sudo virsh net-start default
kvm.setup_step6: Restart the sysmanage-agent service to pick up group membership
kvm.title: KVM/QEMU Virtual Machine Management

lxd.comparison_desc: Both LXD and WSL provide containerized Linux environments, but they differ in several ways:
lxd.comparison_features: Features: LXD supports snapshots, profiles, and clustering; WSL focuses on developer tooling integration
lxd.comparison_images: Images: LXD pulls from the official image server; WSL uses Microsoft Store distributions
lxd.comparison_networking: Networking: LXD containers get routable IPs on lxdbr0; WSL instances share the Windows host network
lxd.comparison_platform: Platform: LXD runs on Linux hosts; WSL runs on Windows hosts
lxd.comparison_tech: Technology: LXD uses Linux containers (LXC); WSL uses a Microsoft compatibility layer
lxd.creation_desc: When creating an LXD container through SysManage:
lxd.creation_step1: SysManage launches the container from the official cloud image
lxd.creation_step2: Container receives an IP address via DHCP from lxdbr0
lxd.creation_step3: The specified user account is created with sudo privileges
lxd.creation_step4: Package repositories are configured based on the distribution
lxd.creation_step5: The sysmanage-agent is installed (via APT, DNF, or Zypper)
lxd.creation_step6: Agent configuration is generated with server connection details
lxd.creation_step7: The agent service starts and connects to SysManage
lxd.creation_step8: Container appears in the Hosts list pending approval
lxd.detect_2: Successful connection to the LXD API
lxd.detect_3: Permission to create and manage containers
lxd.detection_desc: SysManage automatically detects LXD capability by checking:
lxd.detection_role: When detected, the host is assigned the LXD Host role and LXD appears as an available virtualization type.
lxd.distributions_desc: LXD containers can be created using any distribution where the sysmanage-agent is available:
lxd.net_dhcp: DHCP: Containers receive IP addresses automatically from the lxdbr0 bridge
lxd.net_dns: DNS: Containers can resolve external hostnames through the host's DNS configuration
lxd.net_nat: NAT: Outbound connectivity is provided via NAT through the host
lxd.networking_desc: LXD containers use the default bridge network:
lxd.networking_note: For the container to communicate with the SysManage server, the host must have network access to the server, and the container must be able to route through the host.
lxd.overview_desc: LXD is a modern container and virtual machine manager for Linux. SysManage integrates with LXD to provide seamless creation and management of Linux containers directly from the web interface.
lxd.req_bridge: A network bridge must be configured (default: lxdbr0)
lxd.req_initialized: LXD must be initialized (lxd init completed)
lxd.req_installed: LXD must be installed on the host (snap or native package)
lxd.req_user: The sysmanage-agent user must be in the lxd group
lxd.setup_desc: To prepare a host for LXD container management:
lxd.setup_step1: Install LXD: sudo snap install lxd or via package manager
lxd.setup_step2: Initialize LXD: sudo lxd init (accept defaults or customize)
lxd.setup_step3: Add agent user to lxd group: sudo usermod -aG lxd sysmanage-agent
lxd.setup_step4: Restart the sysmanage-agent service to pick up group membership
lxd.title: LXD Container Management

manage.delete_desc: Permanently removes the child host and all its data. This action cannot be undone.
manage.delete_warning_desc: Deleting a child host permanently removes all data stored within it. For WSL, this runs 'wsl --unregister' which completely removes the distribution. Ensure you have backups of any important data before deletion.

troubleshooting.issue10_sol1: Verify the default network is active: virsh net-list
troubleshooting.issue10_sol2: Start the network if needed: virsh net-start default
troubleshooting.issue10_sol4: Verify virbr0 bridge exists: ip addr show virbr0
troubleshooting.issue11_sol2: Check libvirtd is running and the virsh command works
troubleshooting.issue11_sol3: Ensure sysmanage-agent user has libvirt group membership
troubleshooting.issue11_sol4: Restart sysmanage-agent after adding to libvirt group
troubleshooting.issue12_sol3: Verify DNS resolution works inside the VM
troubleshooting.issue12_sol4: Check if package installation failed due to repository access issues
troubleshooting.issue1_sol1: Verify the parent host has network connectivity
troubleshooting.issue1_sol2: Check that the distribution is enabled in Settings
troubleshooting.issue1_sol3: Ensure sufficient disk space on the parent host
troubleshooting.issue1_sol4: Review the error message in the child host status
troubleshooting.issue2_desc: This is normal behavior. Use SysManage's reboot function to restart the Windows host, then retry creating child hosts.
troubleshooting.issue3_sol1: Verify the child host is running (not stopped)
troubleshooting.issue3_sol2: Check that the sysmanage-agent service is running inside the child
troubleshooting.issue3_sol3: Verify network connectivity from the child to the SysManage server
troubleshooting.issue3_sol4: Review the agent logs for connection errors
troubleshooting.issue4_desc: Click on the child host to view the error details. Common causes include:
troubleshooting.issue5_desc: If LXD container creation fails, check the following:
troubleshooting.issue5_sol1: Verify LXD is initialized: lxc list should work without errors
troubleshooting.issue5_sol2: Check lxdbr0 network exists: lxc network list
troubleshooting.issue5_sol3: Ensure sysmanage-agent user is in the lxd group: groups sysmanage-agent
troubleshooting.issue6_desc: If the container starts but has no network connectivity:
troubleshooting.issue6_sol1: Verify lxdbr0 is running: lxc network show lxdbr0
troubleshooting.issue6_sol2: Check container network config: lxc config show CONTAINER
troubleshooting.issue6_sol3: Verify DHCP is working: lxc exec CONTAINER -- ip addr
troubleshooting.issue6_sol4: Try restarting the network: lxc restart CONTAINER
troubleshooting.issue7_desc: If a host with LXD installed doesn't show the LXD Host role:
troubleshooting.issue7_sol1: Verify LXD socket exists and is accessible
troubleshooting.issue7_sol2: Check sysmanage-agent user has lxd group membership
troubleshooting.issue7_sol3: Restart sysmanage-agent after adding to lxd group
troubleshooting.issue7_sol4: Wait for the next role detection cycle or restart the agent
troubleshooting.issue8_desc: This is expected behavior. LXD containers run unprivileged by default for security. The agent will report its capabilities accordingly. The container can still be fully managed, but some privileged operations (like installing certain packages) may require configuration changes.
troubleshooting.issue8_title: LXD Container Agent Shows Not Privileged
troubleshooting.issue9_sol1: Verify KVM is enabled: virsh list --all should work without errors
troubleshooting.issue9_sol2: Check libvirt is running: sudo systemctl status libvirtd
troubleshooting.issue9_sol3: Ensure sysmanage-agent user is in the libvirt group: groups sysmanage-agent
troubleshooting.issue9_sol5: Check the cloud image URL is accessible from the host
troubleshooting.title: Troubleshooting

deploy.automatic_ports_description: When deploying or enabling a firewall, SysManage automatically opens the following ports:
deploy.description: The Deploy Firewall operation installs and configures firewall software on hosts that don't have it installed or enabled.

deploy.step1: Navigate to the host detail page for the target system

disable.description: The Disable Firewall operation stops the firewall service, allowing all traffic through the host.

disable.warning_description: Disabling the firewall removes network protection and exposes all services to the network. Only disable firewalls in controlled environments or when troubleshooting network connectivity issues.

enable.description: The Enable Firewall operation starts the firewall service on a host where firewall software is installed but disabled.

enable.step3: The firewall service will start and required ports will be opened
enable.step4: Firewall status will update to "Enabled" in real-time

restart.description: The Restart Firewall operation stops and starts the firewall service, reloading configuration and rules.
restart.step1: Navigate to the host detail page

roles.example_description: Here's an example of creating a typical web server firewall role:

roles.port_config_udp: UDP: Enable to allow UDP traffic on this port

approvals.description: Scripts marked requires_approval dispatch into the pending_approval state and create a paired ApprovalRequest. An authorized reviewer either approves (the linked execution promotes to queued and dispatches) or rejects with an optional reason (the linked execution flips to rejected). Approvals are scoped per-execution, not per-script, so reuse is explicit.
approvals.title: Approval Workflows

architecture.description: The engine is a Cython-compiled binary (automation_engine.so) loaded by the open-source server's module_loader at startup. It exports the same get_module_info() + get_automation_router() contract as the other Pro+ modules, so mount_automation_routes() wires it under /api/v1/automation alongside the 402 stub routes.
architecture.title: Architecture

deploy_plan.description: Every execution produces a deploy plan that the agent runs step-by-step: write the rendered script body to a tempfile, invoke the right interpreter, then clean up. The plan is wire-compatible with apply_deployment_plan so the same agent handler runs both Pro+ and ad-hoc paths:
deploy_plan.title: Deploy Plan Shape

execution.description: A single POST dispatches one rendered script body to many hosts. Each host gets its own ScriptExecutionHostResult tuple tracking start / completion timestamps, return code, and captured output. The overall ScriptExecution.status rolls up from the per-host states (all-succeeded → succeeded; any-failed → failed; etc.).
execution.title: Multi-host Execution

feature_codes.description: Endpoints in this engine are gated by these feature codes (returned 402 by the open-source server when the module isn't loaded):
feature_codes.title: Feature Codes

header.description: Server-side script automation: a versioned saved-script library, multi-host execution with per-host result tracking, multi-shell support, cron-style scheduling, and approval workflows for privileged scripts. Migrates ~328 lines of agent-side script orchestration to a Cython engine so the agent retains only a thin execution shim.
header.title: Automation Engine

automation_engine.meta_description: Saved-script library with version history, multi-host execution, multi-shell support, scheduled triggers, and approval workflows for privileged scripts.

opensource.adhoc: Community Edition: ad-hoc one-shot script runs against a single host via backend/services/script_plan_builder.py
opensource.approvals: Professional+: scripts flagged requires_approval queue an ApprovalRequest reviewers must clear before the execution dispatches
opensource.library: Professional+: persisted, versioned saved-script library (every edit snapshots the prior body for audit / rollback)
opensource.multihost: Professional+: queue one execution against many hosts; per-host result rolls up into an aggregate status
opensource.parameters: Professional+: typed parameter declarations (string / integer / boolean / float / secret) with validation + ${name} substitution
opensource.scheduling: Professional+: cron-triggered scheduled executions with last-run / next-run tracking
opensource.title: Open Source vs Professional+

overview.description: The Automation Engine owns every aspect of script execution: storing the script, tracking its version history, validating typed parameters, picking the right shell per host, dispatching to many hosts in one call, and capturing the per-host stdout/stderr. The agent's role shrinks to running the deploy plan the server hands it via the existing apply_deployment_plan handler.
overview.title: Overview

parameters.description: Each script declares a list of ScriptParameter entries with a typed signature (string, integer, boolean, float, secret), a required flag, and an optional default. Submitted values are type-coerced and required-checked before the script body's ${name} placeholders are substituted left-to-right. Unknown parameter names are rejected; unknown placeholders are left untouched.
parameters.title: Parameters & Substitution

scheduling.description: A ScheduledExecution binds a saved script + a host list + a cron expression. The engine validates the 5-field cron form on insert; the dispatcher polls and fires matching schedules, recording last_run and last_status. Schedules can be disabled in place without deleting the record.
scheduling.title: Scheduled Execution

scripts.description: A SavedScript bundles a name, shell, body, typed parameter declarations, timeout, and an optional requires_approval flag. Updating an existing script bumps its version and snapshots the previous body into the version-history table; older versions remain executable for rollback / audit replays.
scripts.title: Saved Scripts & Versioning

shells.description: Supported shells: bash, zsh, sh, ksh, powershell, cmd. The engine validates the script's declared shell against each target host's advertised shells (or per-platform defaults) before queueing; hosts that don't support the script's shell appear in the execution's skipped_hosts list rather than being dispatched and failing at the agent.
shells.title: Multi-shell Support

architecture.description: The engine is a Cython-compiled binary (av_management_engine.so) loaded by the open-source server's module_loader at startup. It exports the same get_module_info() + get_av_management_router() contract as the other Pro+ modules, so the open-source server's mount_av_management_routes() wrapper can mount it alongside the 402 stubs without conditionals.

The engine layers an AvPolicy abstraction on top so an operator can define a single named policy (cadence + schedule + product) and apply it to any number of hosts.

compliance.description: Compares each host's role-derived expected port set against what FirewallStatus.tcp_open_ports reports as actually open. A host is compliant iff it has a status row AND actual_ports ⊇ expected_ports.

conflicts.description: Before any plan is queued, detect_rule_conflicts() walks the flattened rule list and surfaces inconsistent intent:

header.description: Server-side, multi-platform firewall rule generation. Builds UFW, firewalld, pf, ipfw, npf, Windows Firewall, and macOS socketfilterfw plans from a unified PortRule schema. Adds role assignment, fleet policy deployment, rule conflict detection, and compliance reporting on top of the open-source basics.
header.title: Firewall Orchestration Engine

lockout.description: Every build_*_rules call always re-permits the management ports first — SSH (22), DNS (53), DHCP (67), plus any agent communication ports passed in host_info["agent_ports"], plus RDP (3389) on Windows. Removal builders skip preserved ports silently and surface them in skipped_preserved_ports so the operator can audit. This is non-bypassable; the engine refuses to emit a plan that would lock the agent out.
lockout.title: Lockout Protection

overview.description: The Firewall Orchestration Engine generates per-platform firewall configurations server-side and ships them to the agent as deploy plans. The agent runs the plan via the generic apply_deployment_plan handler — no firewall business logic lives on the agent (only the read-only firewall_collector.py stays for status reporting). All seven supported flavors share a single PortRule schema with source restrictions, in/out direction, and action (allow/deny).
overview.title: Overview

architecture.description: The engine is a Cython-compiled binary (fleet_engine.so) loaded by the open-source server's module_loader at startup. It exports the same get_module_info() + get_fleet_router() contract as the other Pro+ modules, so mount_fleet_routes() wires it under /api/v1/fleet alongside the 402 stub routes.
architecture.title: Architecture

bulk.description: Eight operation types: run_script, deploy_file, service_control, install_package, remove_package, reboot, shutdown, and apply_deployment_plan. Each BulkOperation record tracks per-host status and rolls up to an aggregate.
bulk.title: Bulk Operations

feature_codes.description: Endpoints in this engine are gated by these feature codes (returned 402 by the open-source server when the module isn't loaded):
feature_codes.title: Feature Codes

groups.description: A HostGroup bundles an explicit host list, dynamic-criteria, and an optional parent group. Membership re-evaluates on every resolve_group_hosts call. The engine rejects parent assignments that would form a cycle, and reparents a deleted group's children to the deleted group's parent.
groups.title: Host Groups

header.description: Server-side fleet management: bulk operations across selectable host sets, host groups with parent/child hierarchies and dynamic-criteria membership, rolling deployments with batched rollout windows and failure thresholds, scheduled fleet operations, and per-operation progress tracking.
header.title: Fleet Engine

fleet_engine.meta_description: Bulk operations, host groups with parent/child hierarchy and dynamic-criteria membership, rolling deployments with failure thresholds, and per-operation progress tracking.

opensource.expand: Community Edition: simple expand-into-per-host plans via backend/services/bulk_op_planner.py (no persistence, no batching, explicit host_ids only)
opensource.groups: Professional+: persistent host groups with parent/child hierarchy and dynamic-criteria membership
opensource.rolling: Professional+: rolling deployments with batched rollout windows + automatic halt on excess failures
opensource.selector: Professional+: HostSelector filter DSL — by tag, platform, group, approval status, or arbitrary criteria with equals / contains / in / matches ops
opensource.title: Open Source vs Professional+
opensource.tracking: Professional+: persisted BulkOperation records, per-host result tracking, aggregated OperationProgress snapshots

overview.description: The Fleet Engine adds operation-at-scale primitives on top of the open-source single-host APIs: a HostSelector filter DSL that resolves to a list of host IDs, persisted BulkOperation records with per-host result tracking, RollingDeployment with configurable batch size and failure-threshold halt, and HostGroup with optional dynamic-criteria membership that re-evaluates on every selector resolution.
overview.title: Overview

rolling.description: A RollingDeployment partitions the resolved host set into batches of batch_size, dispatching one batch at a time. The engine halts when failures exceed failure_threshold_pct. Operators can pause / resume / cancel mid-flight.
rolling.title: Rolling Deployments

scheduling.description: A ScheduledFleetOperation binds an operation type + selector + cron expression. The selector resolves on each fire, so a schedule that targets tags=[prod] automatically picks up newly-tagged hosts.
scheduling.title: Scheduled Fleet Operations

selector.description: A HostSelector is a compound filter combining explicit host IDs with optional criteria (AND-joined). Convenience shortcuts (platforms, tags, groups, approval_status) compile into equivalent criteria before evaluation.
selector.title: Host Selector DSL

overview.description: The Reboot Orchestration module provides a safe, automated workflow for rebooting parent hosts that have running child hosts (WSL or LXD containers). Instead of abruptly rebooting and risking data loss in containers, the orchestrator follows a controlled sequence to ensure all children are cleanly stopped before the reboot and automatically restarted afterward.
overview.title: Overview

requirements.agent: SysManage agent running on the parent host with active connectivity
requirements.children: Parent host must have at least one running child host (WSL or LXD)
requirements.license: Professional or Enterprise license with container_engine module enabled
requirements.permissions: User must have the REBOOT_HOST security role
requirements.title: Requirements

state_machine.description: Each orchestrated reboot follows a well-defined state machine. The orchestration progresses through these states:

failed.desc: One or more child hosts failed to restart; error details available
failed.label: failed

pending_restart.desc: Parent host is rebooting; waiting for agent to reconnect
pending_restart.label: pending_restart

rebooting.desc: All children stopped; reboot command issued to parent host
rebooting.label: rebooting

restarting.desc: Agent reconnected; start commands sent for previously-running children
restarting.label: restarting

shutting_down.desc: Stop commands sent to all running child hosts; waiting for confirmation
shutting_down.label: shutting_down

state_machine.title: Orchestration State Machine

timeouts.agent_reconnect: Agent reconnect: The orchestration transitions from "rebooting" to "restarting" when the parent's agent sends its first heartbeat after reboot
timeouts.audit_logging: Audit logging: All orchestration events are recorded in the audit log for compliance and troubleshooting
timeouts.description: The orchestration service includes built-in safeguards to handle failures at any stage:
timeouts.partial_failure: Partial failure: If some children fail to restart, the orchestration completes with an error message indicating which children failed
timeouts.shutdown_timeout: Shutdown timeout: If child hosts do not stop within the configured timeout (default 120 seconds), the reboot proceeds anyway
timeouts.title: Timeout and Error Handling

defense_in_depth.description: Every protected operation in SysManage uses a two-layer security validation approach:

layer1.description: UI elements (buttons, menus, actions) are hidden or disabled based on user permissions. This prevents users from attempting unauthorized actions and provides a better user experience.
layer1.title: 🖥️ Frontend Layer

layer2.description: API endpoints validate user permissions before executing any operation. Even if the frontend is bypassed, the backend enforces security with HTTP 403 errors for unauthorized access.

defense_in_depth.title: Defense-in-Depth Architecture

why.description: The frontend layer cannot be trusted for security enforcement since it runs in the user's browser and can be manipulated. The backend layer provides the actual security guarantee. Together, they offer both security (backend) and usability (frontend).
